name: LED
tagline: The simplest output component — lights up when current flows through it
description: An LED (Light Emitting Diode) is a semiconductor device that emits light when current flows through it in the forward direction. It is the first component almost every maker uses with Arduino. LEDs are polarized — the longer leg (anode) connects to positive voltage, the shorter leg (cathode) connects to ground. Always use a current-limiting resistor (220Ω typical) in series to prevent burning it out.

pinout.name: Positive / Long Leg
pinout.desc: Connect to digital output pin via 220Ω resistor — HIGH turns LED on

wiringNote: Resistor value formula: R = (Vsupply − Vf) / If. For a red LED on 5V: R = (5 − 2) / 0.02 = 150Ω. Use 220Ω as a safe standard value for most colors at 5V.
libraryName: No library needed (use built-in digitalWrite / analogWrite)
commonMistakes: Connecting LED without a resistor → LED burns out immediately
Reversing polarity (cathode to +) → LED doesn't light and may be damaged
Using a non-PWM pin with analogWrite() for fading → doesn't work
Assuming all LEDs have the same forward voltage — blue/white LEDs need more voltage than red
